_meta.authoringNotes: Pathology and classification per Bajema et al. 2018 ISN/RPS revised classification (Kidney Int PMID 29459092). Treatment guidance per KDIGO 2024 Glomerular Diseases lupus nephritis module, EULAR/ERA-EDTA 2019 (PMID 31511301), ACR 2024 guideline draft. Trial data: BLISS-LN belimumab (Furie 2020 PMID 32885669), AURORA voclosporin (Rovin 2021 PMID 33971170), MMF vs cyclophosphamide (Appel et al. 2009 PMID 19470673). Epidemiology per Hanly et al. Rheumatology 2016.

h1Title: Lupus Nephritis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Lupus nephritis is glomerular injury caused by systemic lupus erythematosus, affecting roughly 40-60% of adults and 60-80% of children with SLE within 5 years of diagnosis. Immune complexes containing anti-double-stranded DNA antibody and nuclear antigens deposit in the glomerulus, triggering complement activation, mesangial proliferation, and capillary wall injury. Patients present with proteinuria, hematuria, hypertension, and rising creatinine, often during a wider lupus flare with rash, arthritis, and fatigue. Untreated proliferative lupus nephritis progresses to kidney failure in 10-30% of patients within 10 years and remains a leading cause of premature death in SLE. Current induction regimens combining mycophenolate mofetil with low-dose glucocorticoids plus belimumab or voclosporin achieve complete renal response in 30-40% and partial response in another 25-35% by 12 months.

keyStats.prevalence: Lupus nephritis develops in 40-60% of adults and 60-80% of children with SLE, with annual incidence of overt nephritis around 0.4-0.7 per 100,000 in Western populations
keyStats.demographics: Female:male ratio 9:1; higher prevalence and severity in Black, Hispanic, Asian, and Indigenous populations
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at lupus nephritis onset 25-35 years; pediatric SLE often develops nephritis within the first year
keyStats.globalCases: Roughly 1.5-3 million people worldwide; lupus nephritis is the leading cause of end-stage kidney disease attributable to autoimmune disease

definition: Lupus nephritis (ICD-10: M32.14, N08) is glomerular injury secondary to systemic lupus erythematosus, caused by deposition of circulating immune complexes containing anti-nuclear and anti-DNA antibodies in the kidney. The disease is histologically heterogeneous and is classified by the 2018 revised International Society of Nephrology/Renal Pathology Society (ISN/RPS) system into six classes: class I (minimal mesangial), class II (mesangial proliferative), class III (focal, less than 50% of glomeruli involved), class IV (diffuse, 50% or more of glomeruli involved, the most severe proliferative form), class V (membranous), and class VI (advanced sclerosing, more than 90% globally sclerosed). Classes III and IV are subclassified as active (A), chronic (C), or both, and class V may coexist with III or IV. Immunofluorescence typically shows a 'full-house' pattern (IgG, IgA, IgM, C3, and C1q) — a feature highly suggestive of lupus when present. The disease is managed jointly by nephrology and rheumatology, with renal pathology, obstetrics, and dermatology input as needed.

typesClassification.type: Class I — Minimal mesangial lupus nephritis
typesClassification.description: Normal light microscopy with mesangial immune deposits on immunofluorescence and electron microscopy. Asymptomatic; no specific treatment beyond SLE control.

typesClassification.type: Class II — Mesangial proliferative lupus nephritis
typesClassification.description: Pure mesangial hypercellularity. Microscopic hematuria and low-grade proteinuria; renal prognosis excellent with hydroxychloroquine and treatment of the underlying lupus.

typesClassification.type: Class III — Focal lupus nephritis (A, A/C, C)
typesClassification.description: Subendothelial deposits with proliferation in under 50% of glomeruli. Active lesions cause hematuria, proteinuria, and falling eGFR; requires immunosuppressive induction.

typesClassification.type: Class IV — Diffuse lupus nephritis (S/G, A/C)
typesClassification.description: Subendothelial deposits and proliferation in 50% or more of glomeruli — the commonest and most severe proliferative form. Frequent nephrotic syndrome, hypertension, and acute kidney injury.
Subepithelial immune deposits causing thickened glomerular capillary walls. Presents with nephrotic-range proteinuria; may coexist with class III or IV (mixed class V/III or V/IV).

typesClassification.type: Class VI — Advanced sclerosing lupus nephritis
typesClassification.description: More than 90% of glomeruli globally sclerosed. Represents end-stage disease; immunosuppression no longer beneficial and renal replacement therapy is the focus.

primarySymptoms: Foamy urine and ankle swelling reflecting proteinuria above 0.5-3.5 g/day, the most common presenting feature.
Cola-coloured or visibly bloody urine during severe class III or IV flares, with dysmorphic red blood cells and red cell casts on microscopy.
Bilateral lower extremity oedema, periorbital swelling, and weight gain from nephrotic syndrome in class V or mixed disease.
New or worsening hypertension, often resistant to single-agent therapy, in 30-50% of patients at presentation.
Fatigue, malaise, and reduced exercise tolerance from anemia of chronic disease, uremia, or active lupus.
Malar (butterfly) rash, photosensitive cutaneous lupus, oral ulcers, alopecia, and arthralgia signalling concurrent systemic lupus activity.
Pleuritic chest pain and serositis with pleural or pericardial effusions during severe SLE flares.
Reduced urine output, nausea, pruritus, and asterixis in patients reaching advanced chronic kidney disease.
earlyWarningSigns: New proteinuria on dipstick or urine protein-to-creatinine ratio above 200 mg/g in a known SLE patient
Persistent microscopic hematuria with dysmorphic red blood cells on phase-contrast microscopy
Falling complement (C3 or C4) and rising anti-double-stranded DNA titres on routine SLE monitoring
New hypertension developing under age 40 in a patient with known SLE or a positive antinuclear antibody
Pregnancy planning or early pregnancy in a woman with known SLE — pre-conception kidney assessment is essential
emergencySigns: Rapidly rising serum creatinine, oliguria, and active urinary sediment — possible rapidly progressive crescentic lupus nephritis
Hypertensive emergency with headache, seizures, or visual change
Heavy hematuria with passage of clots or severe flank pain (rare but reported with renal vein thrombosis in nephrotic SLE)
Severe pulmonary symptoms with hypoxia in a patient with lupus nephritis — exclude diffuse alveolar haemorrhage or pulmonary embolism
New focal neurology, seizures, or psychosis in a patient on immunosuppression — exclude CNS lupus, infection, or PRES

causes.description: Lupus nephritis is the kidney manifestation of SLE, an autoimmune disease driven by autoantibodies against nuclear antigens (dsDNA, histones, nucleosomes, Sm, Ro). Immune complexes deposit in the kidney mesangium, subendothelium, and subepithelium, activating complement and recruiting inflammatory cells.

Anti-dsDNA antibodies are present in 60-80% of patients with active lupus nephritis. They bind nucleosomes deposited in the glomerular basement membrane, fix complement, and amplify glomerular injury. Rising titres predict renal flares.
Immune complexes activate the classical complement pathway, consuming C3 and C4. Low complement levels at SLE diagnosis carry a 2-3 fold higher risk of nephritis development within 5 years.
HLA-DR2, HLA-DR3, and complement deficiencies (C1q, C2, C4) confer the strongest genetic risk. Genome-wide studies have identified over 100 SLE susceptibility loci including STAT4, IRF5, and ITGAM.

Estrogens, ultraviolet light, smoking, Epstein-Barr virus infection, and silica exposure precipitate lupus and renal flares. The 9:1 female predominance and pregnancy-associated flares reflect the role of sex hormones.

Hydralazine, procainamide, anti-TNF biologics, and minocycline can cause lupus-like disease, occasionally with mild nephritis. The disease typically remits within 6 months of stopping the offending drug.

riskFactors.factor: Confirmed diagnosis of systemic lupus erythematosus
riskFactors.description: All cases of lupus nephritis occur in patients with SLE. 40-60% of adults and 60-80% of children with SLE develop kidney involvement within 5 years.
9:1 female:male predominance in adults. Disease in men is often more severe with higher rates of nephritis and end-stage kidney disease.

riskFactors.factor: Black, Hispanic, Asian, or Indigenous ancestry
riskFactors.description: Lupus nephritis prevalence is 2-3 times higher and progression to kidney failure 2-4 times faster than in White populations. APOL1 high-risk variants worsen outcomes in patients of West African ancestry.
Persistently elevated anti-dsDNA titre confers a 5-10 fold higher risk of nephritis flare within 12 months. Routine monitoring guides preemptive escalation.
Low C3 and C4 are markers of active immune complex disease and a 2-3 fold higher risk of nephritis flare.
Both accelerate proteinuria and eGFR loss in established nephritis. Weight reduction and blood pressure control under 130/80 mmHg are part of standard care.

Pregnancy doubles the risk of lupus flare and is high-risk in active nephritis. Pre-pregnancy planning with quiescent disease for at least 6 months on safe medications reduces flares and adverse outcomes.
Hydroxychloroquine reduces flare risk by 50% and improves renal outcomes. Stopping the drug is the single most common modifiable risk factor for nephritis flare.

affectedDemographics: Lupus nephritis affects an estimated 1.5-3 million people worldwide, mostly women in their reproductive years.
Black women in the United States have lupus nephritis incidence 3-4 times higher than White women, with 2-fold higher progression to ESKD (Hanly et al. 2016).
Hispanic and Indigenous populations of the Americas show similar elevated risk and a more aggressive disease course.
Pediatric SLE: more than 60% of children develop nephritis within 1 year of SLE diagnosis; prognosis is improved by adult-protocol immunosuppression.
Pregnancy: women with active nephritis at conception have 2-3 fold higher rates of pre-eclampsia, preterm birth, and fetal loss.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with routine SLE surveillance — urinalysis, urine protein-to-creatinine ratio, blood pressure, serum creatinine, complement (C3 and C4), and anti-dsDNA at every clinic visit. Suspected lupus nephritis is defined by KDIGO 2024 and ACR criteria as proteinuria above 0.5 g/day (or urine protein-to-creatinine ratio above 500 mg/g) and/or active urinary sediment (red cells, red cell casts) in a patient meeting SLE classification criteria. A kidney biopsy is recommended in nearly all patients with proteinuria above 0.5 g/day to confirm the diagnosis, define the ISN/RPS class, score activity and chronicity, and exclude alternative or coexisting disease (lupus podocytopathy, APOL1 nephropathy, thrombotic microangiopathy). Light microscopy assesses glomerular and tubulointerstitial changes; immunofluorescence usually shows the characteristic full-house pattern (IgG, IgA, IgM, C3, C1q); electron microscopy localises deposits (mesangial, subendothelial, subepithelial). Class III, IV, V, and mixed lesions carry distinct treatment implications. Adjunctive investigations include antiphospholipid antibody screen (anticardiolipin, anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-I, lupus anticoagulant), thrombotic microangiopathy markers (haptoglobin, LDH, blood film), and viral hepatitis screen before immunosuppression. The activity and chronicity indices (NIH or modified) guide intensity of induction therapy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects active urinary sediment — dysmorphic red blood cells, red cell casts, leukocyte casts
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Provide a fresh midstream urine sample. The lab examines a centrifuged sediment under phase-contrast microscopy. Result the same day.

diagnosticTests.test: Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio and 24-hour urine collection
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies proteinuria, the major driver of nephron loss and a key trial endpoint
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Spot first-morning urine for ratio; 24-hour collection if quantitation is needed in pregnancy or clinical trials. Result within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies kidney function and monitors response to therapy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw, same-day result. Repeated monthly during induction therapy.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum complement C3 and C4 with anti-dsDNA antibody
diagnosticTests.purpose: Markers of disease activity; falling complement and rising anti-dsDNA predict flares
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw, results within 5-7 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Percutaneous kidney biopsy with ISN/RPS classification
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms diagnosis, defines class (I-VI), and grades activity/chronicity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Day-case procedure under local anaesthetic with ultrasound guidance. Two or three small cores are taken with a 16-18 gauge needle. Patients lie flat for 4-6 hours afterwards. Histology reported within 7-10 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies coexisting antiphospholipid syndrome and thrombotic risk
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw for lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, and anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-I. Lupus anticoagulant requires repeat testing after 12 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes obstruction, confirms two normally sized kidneys, screens for renal vein thrombosis in nephrotic syndrome
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard ultrasound, 20-30 minutes, no contrast or fasting required.

treatmentOverview: Lupus nephritis treatment follows the KDIGO 2024 and ACR/EULAR 2024 guidelines and proceeds in two phases: induction to achieve renal response, followed by maintenance to prevent flare. All patients receive lifelong hydroxychloroquine (5 mg/kg/day, max 400 mg) unless contraindicated, blood pressure control under 120/80 mmHg with renin-angiotensin blockade, SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin or empagliflozin 10 mg daily) once stable, and statin therapy if dyslipidemic. For active class III/IV (with or without V) disease, induction combines glucocorticoids (now lower-dose: pulse methylprednisolone 250-500 mg for 1-3 days then oral prednisolone 0.4-0.5 mg/kg/day rapidly tapered to 5 mg by month 3) with either mycophenolate mofetil (target 2-3 g/day) or low-dose intravenous cyclophosphamide (Euro-Lupus regimen, 500 mg every 2 weeks for 6 doses). Addition of belimumab (10 mg/kg IV monthly after weekly loading) — based on BLISS-LN — or voclosporin (23.7 mg twice daily; based on AURORA) significantly increases complete renal response rates from around 25% to 40% at 12 months. For pure class V disease, mycophenolate plus a calcineurin inhibitor (tacrolimus 0.05-0.1 mg/kg/day) is preferred. Maintenance is mycophenolate mofetil (1-2 g/day) or azathioprine (2 mg/kg/day) for at least 3 years, with prednisolone tapered to under 5 mg/day. Refractory disease is treated with rituximab, obinutuzumab, or anti-CD38 daratumumab in trials. Severe refractory or end-stage disease may need plasma exchange or kidney transplantation.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydroxychloroquine 5 mg/kg/day (max 400 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Backbone therapy for all SLE patients regardless of nephritis activity. Reduces flare rate, improves renal response to induction, and protects against atherosclerosis. Annual ophthalmology screening after 5 years for retinopathy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 50% reduction in lupus flares; 30-40% lower risk of progressing to ESKD in observational cohorts

medicalTreatments.name: Mycophenolate mofetil 2-3 g/day in divided doses
medicalTreatments.description: First-line induction for class III/IV and class V lupus nephritis. Targets activated lymphocytes. Monitor for cytopenias and infection. Avoid in pregnancy — teratogenic.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete plus partial renal response in 55-65% at 6 months in ALMS trial; non-inferior to cyclophosphamide with better safety profile

medicalTreatments.name: Cyclophosphamide (Euro-Lupus low-dose IV: 500 mg every 2 weeks for 6 doses, or NIH regimen 0.5-1 g/m2 monthly for 6 months)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative induction agent, especially in rapidly progressive crescentic disease or class IV with severe activity. Euro-Lupus regimen has reduced gonadotoxicity and infection compared with the high-dose NIH regimen.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable 5-year renal survival to mycophenolate; preferred in severe or rapidly progressive disease

medicalTreatments.name: Belimumab 10 mg/kg IV monthly after weekly loading (or 200 mg subcutaneous weekly)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-BAFF monoclonal antibody added to mycophenolate or cyclophosphamide induction in class III/IV/V disease. Reduces flares and improves complete renal response.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: BLISS-LN trial: 43% vs 32% complete renal response at 104 weeks; 49% reduction in renal-related events

medicalTreatments.name: Voclosporin 23.7 mg twice daily
medicalTreatments.description: Novel calcineurin inhibitor with improved pharmacokinetics versus cyclosporine and tacrolimus. Added to mycophenolate and steroids; FDA-approved 2021 for active lupus nephritis based on AURORA trial.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: AURORA: 41% vs 23% complete renal response at 52 weeks; consistent benefit at 30 months in AURORA 2 extension

medicalTreatments.name: Prednisolone 0.4-0.5 mg/kg/day tapered to ≤5 mg/day by 3 months
medicalTreatments.description: Backbone of induction. Recent trials and guidelines favour lower starting doses to reduce long-term toxicity (osteoporosis, diabetes, cataract, infection). Pulse methylprednisolone 250-500 mg for 1-3 days often used at outset.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Lower-dose regimens achieve comparable response with substantially less infection and steroid toxicity in NIH and Euro-Lupus protocols

medicalTreatments.name: Azathioprine 2 mg/kg/day for maintenance
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative to mycophenolate for maintenance, particularly in women of childbearing potential or planning pregnancy. Check TPMT activity before starting; monitor for cytopenia and hepatitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Maintenance of remission in 70-80% at 3 years; slightly higher flare rate than mycophenolate in MAINTAIN trial

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab 1 g day 0 and 14 (off-label, refractory disease)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-CD20 monoclonal used for refractory disease, recurrent flare, or where mycophenolate and cyclophosphamide are contraindicated. Often combined with mycophenolate for B-cell depletion.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 55-70% renal response in refractory cohorts; LUNAR RCT did not show superiority over placebo on top of standard induction but real-world cohorts support use

surgicalOptions.name: Kidney transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Preferred renal replacement modality once eGFR falls below 15 mL/min/1.73 m2. Living-donor and pre-emptive transplantation give the best outcomes. Patients should be in clinical and serological remission for 3-6 months before listing.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year graft survival 80-85% for living-donor and 70-75% for deceased-donor; recurrence of lupus nephritis in the graft 2-10%

surgicalOptions.name: Arteriovenous fistula creation for haemodialysis access
surgicalOptions.description: Performed when eGFR approaches 15-20 mL/min/1.73 m2 to allow timely dialysis access. Brescia-Cimino fistula at the wrist is preferred where vessel calibre allows.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Primary patency 60-70% at 12 months; maturation rate above 80% in non-diabetic patients

alternativeTreatments.name: Multitarget therapy (mycophenolate plus tacrolimus plus prednisolone)
alternativeTreatments.description: Used in some Asian centres for refractory or mixed class V/IV disease. Provides simultaneous T- and B-cell suppression and is supported by Chinese RCT data (Liu et al. 2015).

alternativeTreatments.name: Vitamin D supplementation (1,000-2,000 IU/day)
alternativeTreatments.description: Most SLE patients are vitamin D deficient. Supplementation improves disease activity scores in some small RCTs and is supported by EULAR adjunctive recommendations.

whySeeSpecialist: Lupus nephritis is managed jointly by nephrology and rheumatology. Early specialist referral allows timely biopsy, ISN/RPS classification, and induction therapy — delays beyond 3 months from proteinuria onset are associated with worse renal survival. Refractory or rapidly progressive disease, pregnancy, and end-stage kidney disease should be managed at tertiary centres.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a nephrologist with a percutaneous biopsy programme and a rheumatologist experienced in SLE classification and biologic therapy. For pregnant or pregnancy-planning patients, identify a high-risk obstetric unit experienced in lupus nephritis. Ask whether the centre uses lower-dose glucocorticoid protocols and has access to belimumab and voclosporin.
hospitalCriteria: Joint nephrology-rheumatology clinic for lupus patients
On-site kidney biopsy programme with renal pathology and electron microscopy
Access to belimumab, voclosporin, mycophenolate, cyclophosphamide, and rituximab
keyFacilities: Tertiary nephrology centres with biopsy and transplant programmes
Lupus clinics with combined nephrology-rheumatology input
Renal pathology with full immunofluorescence and electron microscopy
preventionStrategies: Maintain lifelong hydroxychloroquine 5 mg/kg/day in all SLE patients to halve flare risk and improve renal outcomes.
Use broad-spectrum sun protection (SPF 50+) and avoid tanning beds — UV exposure precipitates SLE and lupus nephritis flares.
Stop smoking; smoking doubles lupus disease activity scores and accelerates kidney decline.
Plan pregnancy when nephritis has been quiescent for at least 6 months on safe medications (hydroxychloroquine, azathioprine, tacrolimus, low-dose steroids).
Vaccinate against influenza, pneumococcus, SARS-CoV-2, HPV, and shingles before immunosuppression where possible.
Screen all SLE patients with urinalysis, urine protein-to-creatinine ratio, blood pressure, complement, and anti-dsDNA at every clinic visit.
lifestyleModifications: Take hydroxychloroquine every day even when feeling well — adherence is the strongest modifiable predictor of remission.
Use SPF 50+ broad-spectrum sunscreen daily on exposed skin.
Stop smoking and limit alcohol to under 14 units per week.
Maintain a healthy body mass index of 18.5-24.9 kg/m2 and treat obesity with diet, exercise, and weight-loss medications where appropriate.
Restrict sodium to under 2 g/day during active nephritis and oedematous states.
Discuss reliable contraception with the rheumatology team while on teratogenic medications (mycophenolate, cyclophosphamide).

recommended: Mediterranean-style diet rich in vegetables, fruits, whole grains, fish, and olive oil
Vitamin-D-rich foods (oily fish, fortified dairy, egg yolks) plus supplementation to keep 25-OH-vitamin-D above 30 ng/mL
Low-sodium intake under 2 g/day during active nephritis
Adequate calcium (1,000-1,200 mg/day) to offset steroid-induced bone loss
avoid: Excess sodium and processed foods that worsen oedema and hypertension
Alfalfa sprouts, which contain L-canavanine and can precipitate lupus flares
High-sugar, high-saturated-fat diets that worsen steroid-induced metabolic syndrome
Grapefruit and grapefruit juice while on tacrolimus or voclosporin — they raise drug levels

exerciseGuidelines: Aerobic exercise at moderate intensity (150 minutes per week) reduces fatigue and improves cardiovascular outcomes in SLE. Add two resistance sessions to offset steroid-induced muscle loss and osteoporosis. Avoid extreme exertion during active flares. Patients with eGFR under 30 mL/min/1.73 m2 should adjust intensity and discuss exercise plans with their renal team.
dailyManagement: Take hydroxychloroquine, mycophenolate or azathioprine, and prednisolone at the same time each day.
Apply broad-spectrum sunscreen daily and reapply every 2 hours during outdoor activity.
Check home blood pressure twice weekly and report sustained readings above 130/80 mmHg.
Self-monitor for new oedema, foamy urine, rashes, joint pain, and unexplained fevers.
Attend monthly blood tests during induction (full blood count, creatinine, urine protein, complement, anti-dsDNA, liver enzymes).
Use reliable contraception while on mycophenolate or cyclophosphamide.
prognosis: Prognosis has improved substantially over the past 50 years. Five-year renal survival is now above 90% and 10-year survival 80-85% with modern induction-maintenance regimens. Complete renal response (proteinuria under 0.5 g/day, normal creatinine) at 6-12 months is the strongest predictor of long-term outcome — patients in complete response have under 5% risk of ESKD at 10 years, versus 30-40% in patients with no response. Class IV disease, mixed class IV/V, high chronicity index, hypertension, Black ancestry, and male sex predict worse outcomes. Cardiovascular disease, infection, and ESKD account for most premature deaths. Pregnancy outcomes are good when nephritis has been quiescent for at least 6 months pre-conception. Approximately 10-30% of patients still progress to ESKD within 10-15 years, but kidney transplantation offers excellent results with under 10% recurrence and 5-year graft survival above 80%.
recoveryTimeline: Proteinuria begins to fall within 4-8 weeks of starting induction therapy. Complete renal response (proteinuria under 0.5 g/g and normal creatinine) is achieved by 6-12 months in 25-45% with mycophenolate plus glucocorticoids and 40-50% when belimumab or voclosporin is added. Maintenance therapy continues for at least 3-5 years; relapses occur in 20-40% over 5 years and are managed with reinduction.
complications: End-stage kidney disease requiring dialysis or transplantation in 10-30% within 10-15 years; monitor eGFR every 3 months during active disease.

Treatment-related infections (pneumonia, varicella zoster, opportunistic pathogens including Pneumocystis jirovecii) — vaccinate and provide prophylactic trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole when on high-dose immunosuppression.
Cardiovascular disease (coronary disease, stroke, peripheral vascular disease) — risk doubled to tripled compared with the general population; aggressive risk-factor control needed.
Steroid toxicity: osteoporosis, avascular necrosis (especially femoral head), diabetes, cataract, weight gain — minimised by lower-dose protocols and bone-protective therapy.
Premature gonadal failure with high-dose cyclophosphamide; preserved by Euro-Lupus regimen and GnRH agonist co-therapy.
Thrombotic events in patients with coexisting antiphospholipid syndrome — anticoagulation may be required.

supportResources.name: Lupus Foundation of America
supportResources.description: US patient organisation with disease information, peer support, and trial registry.

supportResources.name: LUPUS UK
supportResources.description: UK charity providing patient support, information leaflets, and helpline for people with SLE and lupus nephritis.

supportResources.name: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases Guideline 2024
supportResources.description: International nephrology guideline; authoritative reference for lupus nephritis management.

supportResources.name: Lupus Research Alliance
supportResources.description: Research foundation funding SLE and lupus nephritis trials, with patient-focused educational resources.

confusedWithConditions.name: IgA Nephropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both produce hematuria and proteinuria, but IgA nephropathy has dominant IgA-only deposits, normal complement, negative ANA and anti-dsDNA, and lacks systemic lupus features (rash, arthritis, serositis). Lupus nephritis shows full-house immunofluorescence and positive lupus serology.

confusedWithConditions.name: ANCA-Associated Vasculitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis with crescents. ANCA vasculitis shows pauci-immune (minimal deposit) histology with positive MPO or PR3 antibodies and often pulmonary or sinus involvement. Lupus nephritis has immune-complex deposits and positive ANA/anti-dsDNA.

confusedWithConditions.name: Membranous Nephropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both can present with nephrotic syndrome and subepithelial deposits. Primary membranous shows isolated IgG4 and positive anti-PLA2R antibodies; lupus class V shows IgG plus IgA, IgM, C3, and C1q ('full-house') and positive lupus serology.

confusedWithConditions.name: Nephrotic Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Nephrotic syndrome is a clinical pattern with multiple causes (minimal change, FSGS, membranous, diabetes). Lupus nephritis classes V and IV can present nephrotic, but additional features — ANA, anti-dsDNA, low complement, full-house biopsy — confirm SLE as the cause.

relatedConditions.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
relatedConditions.relevance: The underlying systemic disease in every case of lupus nephritis; nearly half of SLE patients develop nephritis.

relatedConditions.name: IgA Nephropathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Another common immune-complex glomerular disease in young adults; key differential and biopsy distinction.

relatedConditions.name: Membranous Nephropathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Class V lupus nephritis is the membranous form; primary membranous is the major differential.

relatedConditions.name: ANCA-Associated Vasculitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both cause rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis with crescents; lupus and ANCA overlap syndromes occur.

relatedConditions.name: End-Stage Kidney Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Long-term complication of untreated or refractory lupus nephritis in 10-30% over 10-15 years.

faqs.question: What is lupus nephritis?
faqs.answer: Lupus nephritis is kidney inflammation caused by systemic lupus erythematosus. Immune complexes containing antibodies against the patient's own DNA deposit in the kidney filters, triggering inflammation and scarring. It causes blood and protein in the urine, high blood pressure, and reduced kidney function.

faqs.question: What percentage of lupus patients get lupus nephritis?
faqs.answer: Around 40-60% of adults and 60-80% of children with systemic lupus erythematosus develop lupus nephritis, usually within 5 years of SLE diagnosis. Lupus nephritis is more common and more severe in Black, Hispanic, Asian, and Indigenous populations.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of lupus nephritis?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include foamy urine from heavy protein loss, ankle and facial swelling, cola-coloured urine during severe flares, fatigue, weight gain, and new or worsening high blood pressure. Many patients are detected by routine urinalysis before symptoms develop.

faqs.question: How is lupus nephritis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires proteinuria above 0.5 g/day or active urinary sediment in a patient with SLE, confirmed by a kidney biopsy showing characteristic immune-complex deposits. Supporting tests include serum creatinine, complement C3/C4, anti-double-stranded DNA, and antiphospholipid antibodies.

faqs.question: Is lupus nephritis curable?
faqs.answer: Lupus nephritis is not curable but is highly controllable. With modern induction-maintenance therapy, 80-90% achieve at least partial renal response. Sustained remission can last decades, but flares occur in 20-40% over 5 years and require careful long-term follow-up.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy with lupus nephritis?
faqs.answer: Five-year renal survival is now above 90% and 10-year survival is 80-85% with modern treatment. Cardiovascular disease and infection are the leading causes of premature death. Patients who achieve complete renal response at 12 months have near-normal long-term renal survival.

faqs.question: What classes of lupus nephritis are there?
faqs.answer: The 2018 ISN/RPS classification recognises six classes: I (minimal mesangial), II (mesangial proliferative), III (focal), IV (diffuse, most severe), V (membranous), and VI (advanced sclerosing). Classes III, IV, and mixed III/V or IV/V drive immunosuppressive treatment decisions.

faqs.question: Do I need a kidney biopsy?
faqs.answer: Yes, in nearly all patients with proteinuria above 0.5 g/day or active urinary sediment. The biopsy defines the ISN/RPS class, scores activity and chronicity, and rules out alternative diagnoses. Treatment intensity and prognosis depend on biopsy findings.

faqs.question: What is the role of hydroxychloroquine?
faqs.answer: Hydroxychloroquine (5 mg/kg/day, max 400 mg) is recommended for every patient with SLE regardless of nephritis activity. It halves flare risk, improves response to induction therapy, and reduces cardiovascular events. Annual ophthalmology screening starts after 5 years of treatment.

faqs.question: What is voclosporin?
faqs.answer: Voclosporin is a calcineurin inhibitor FDA-approved in 2021 for active lupus nephritis. Added to mycophenolate and steroids, it produced complete renal response in 41% versus 23% on standard therapy at one year in the AURORA trial, with benefit maintained at 30 months.

faqs.question: Can pregnancy be safe with lupus nephritis?
faqs.answer: Yes, when planned. Conception should be timed for at least 6 months of quiescent nephritis on pregnancy-safe drugs (hydroxychloroquine, azathioprine, tacrolimus, low-dose steroids). Mycophenolate and cyclophosphamide are teratogenic and must be stopped 3 months before conception.

faqs.question: Is lupus nephritis hereditary?
faqs.answer: Lupus nephritis itself is not directly inherited, but susceptibility to SLE has a strong genetic component. First-degree relatives of SLE patients have a 5-15% risk of developing the disease. Genetic risk is highest in those of West African, Hispanic, and Asian ancestry.

faqs.question: What is the best diet for lupus nephritis?
faqs.answer: A Mediterranean-style diet with adequate fruits, vegetables, whole grains, fish, and olive oil supports cardiovascular and renal health. Limit sodium to under 2 g/day during active nephritis. Avoid alfalfa sprouts, which can trigger lupus flares.

faqs.question: Does lupus nephritis cause kidney failure?
faqs.answer: Approximately 10-30% of lupus nephritis patients progress to end-stage kidney disease within 10-15 years, especially class IV/V with high chronicity index, hypertension, or Black ancestry. Modern induction-maintenance therapy reduces this risk and delays progression.

faqs.question: What is the role of belimumab?
faqs.answer: Belimumab is a monoclonal antibody against B-cell activating factor (BAFF), approved as add-on therapy for active lupus nephritis. In the BLISS-LN trial it increased complete renal response from 32% to 43% at 2 years and reduced renal-related events by 49%.

faqs.question: Can men get lupus nephritis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Although SLE is 9 times more common in women, men can develop lupus and lupus nephritis. Male patients often have more severe disease, with higher rates of nephritis at diagnosis, faster progression to kidney failure, and more cardiovascular comorbidity.

faqs.question: Will I need kidney dialysis or a transplant?
faqs.answer: Most patients on modern therapy do not need dialysis or transplant. Around 10-30% progress to end-stage kidney disease in the long term. Transplantation is the preferred option once dialysis is needed, with excellent outcomes and low (2-10%) recurrence of lupus nephritis in the graft.

faqs.question: How often should I see my nephrologist?
faqs.answer: During active induction therapy, plan monthly visits with blood and urine tests. Once in remission, visits every 3 months for the first 2 years, then every 6 months long-term. Pregnant patients need monthly multidisciplinary review.

faqs.question: What infections are most common during treatment?
faqs.answer: Common infections include bacterial pneumonia, urinary tract infection, varicella zoster, and opportunistic pathogens such as Pneumocystis jirovecii. Vaccinate against influenza, pneumococcus, and SARS-CoV-2 before immunosuppression, and use prophylactic trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole during high-dose induction.

faqs.question: What blood pressure target is recommended?
faqs.answer: Most guidelines recommend a target under 130/80 mmHg, with under 120/80 mmHg preferred in proteinuric disease. ACE inhibitors or angiotensin receptor blockers are first-line. SGLT2 inhibitors are added once nephritis is stable, providing additional kidney protection.

faqs.question: Can lupus nephritis come back after remission?
faqs.answer: Yes. Renal flares occur in 20-40% of patients within 5 years, often after stopping hydroxychloroquine, missed mycophenolate, infection, pregnancy, or sun exposure. Most flares respond to reinduction; close monitoring with urine protein and serology detects relapse early.

metaTitle: Lupus Nephritis: Symptoms, Treatment & Prognosis | aihealz
metaDescription: Lupus nephritis: kidney inflammation from SLE. Symptoms, ISN/RPS biopsy classes, voclosporin/belimumab treatment, pregnancy planning, and prognosis explained.
